--- SOURCE 1: The nonlinearity of regulation in biological networks ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-023-00273-w

SUMMARY:
A comparison of the resulting series of approximations of the biological models with appropriate random ensembles revealed that biological regulation tends to be less nonlinear than expected, meaning that higher-order interactions among the regulatory inputs tend to be less pronounced. To that end we specifically ask: (1) how well could biological Boolean models be approximated, that is, faithfully represented with only partial information containing lower levels of nonlinearity relative to that of the original?; (2) is there an optimal level of regulatory nonlinearity, characterized by maximum approximability, that these models may have been selected for by evolution?; and (3) do different classes of biological networks show characteristically different optimal levels of regulatory nonlinearity?

# The nonlinearity of regulation in biological networks

* [Santosh Manicka](#auth-Santosh-Manicka-Aff1)[1](#Aff1)[na1](#na1),
* [Kathleen Johnson](#auth-Kathleen-Johnson-Aff2) 
  [ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-0432-0362](https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0432-0362)[2](#Aff2)[na1](#na1),
* [Michael Levin](#auth-Michael-Levin-Aff1)[1](#Aff1) &
* …
* [David Murrugarra](#auth-David-Murrugarra-Aff2) 
  [ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-9710-5025](https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9710-5025)[2](#Aff2)

[*npj Systems Biology and Applications*](/npjsba)
**volume 9**, Article number: 10 (2023)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

* 7182 Accesses
* 23 Citations
* 19 Altmetric
* [Metrics details](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w/metrics)

## Abstract

The extent to which the components of a biological system are (non)linearly regulated determines how amenable they are to therapy and control. To better understand this property termed “regulatory nonlinearity”, we analyzed a suite of 137 published Boolean network models, containing a variety of complex nonlinear regulatory interactions, using a probabilistic generalization of Boolean logic that George Boole himself had proposed. Leveraging the continuous-nature of this formulation, we used Taylor decomposition to approximate the models with various levels of regulatory nonlinearity. A comparison of the resulting series of approximations of the biological models with appropriate random ensembles revealed that biological regulation tends to be less nonlinear than expected, meaning that higher-order interactions among the regulatory inputs tend to be less pronounced. A further categorical analysis of the biological models revealed that the regulatory nonlinearity of cancer and disease networks could not only be sometimes higher than expected but also be relatively more variable. We show that this variation is caused by differences in the apportioning of information among the various orders of regulatory nonlinearity. Our results suggest that there may have been a weak but discernible selection pressure for biological systems to evolve linear regulation on average, but for certain systems such as cancer, on the other hand, to simultaneously evolve more nonlinear rules.

## Introduction

How nonlinear is the regulation of the components of biological networks? That is, to what extent do the biochemical components of these networks non-independently interact (Fig. [1](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig1)) in influencing downstream processes and network behavior. Research on the “nonlinearity” front has hitherto focused on its various manifestations in the dynamics of biological systems, such as chaos, bifurcation, multistability, synchronization, patterning, dissipation, etc.[1](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR1 "Kapitaniak, T. & Jafari, S. Nonlinear Effects in Life Sciences (Springer, 2018)."), but a characterization of regulatory nonlinearity among the components of the underlying systems that give rise to those phenomena is lacking. A more complete understanding of biological regulatory nonlinearity would not only yield insights into their design principles[2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR2 "Savageau, M. A. Design principles for elementary gene circuits: elements, methods, and examples. Chaos 11, 142–159 (2001).") but also have theoretical implications ranging from canalization to control[3](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR3 "Kadelka, C., Butrie, T.-M., Hilton, E., Kinseth, J. & Serdarevic, H. A meta-analysis of Boolean network models reveals design principles of gene regulatory networks. arXiv 
                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2009.01216
                  
                 (2020)."),[4](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR4 "Borriello, E. & Daniels, B. C. The basis of easy controllability in Boolean networks. Nat. Commun. 12, 5227 (2021).") and practical implications for biomedical therapy, synthetic biology, etc.[1](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR1 "Kapitaniak, T. & Jafari, S. Nonlinear Effects in Life Sciences (Springer, 2018)."),[5](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR5 "Stoof, R. & Goñi-Moreno, Á. Modelling co-translational dimerization for programmable nonlinearity in synthetic biology. J. R. Soc. Interface 17, 20200561 (2020)."). A good example of this concerns the mapping between molecular or genetic information and the resulting system-level anatomical structure and function of an organism. Advances in regenerative medicine and synthetic morphology require rational control of physiological and anatomical outcomes[6](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR6 "Pezzulo, G. & Levin, M. Top-down models in biology: explanation and control of complex living systems above the molecular level. J. R. Soc. Interface 13, 20160555 (2016)."), but progress in genetics and molecular biology produce methods and knowledge targeting the lowest-level cellular hardware. There is no one-to-one mapping from genetic information to tissue- and organ-level structure; similarly, ion channels open and close post-translationally, driving physiological dynamics that are not readily inferred from proteomic or transcriptomic data. System-level properties in biology are often highly emergent, with gene-regulatory or bioelectric circuit dynamics connecting initial state information and transition rules to large-scale structure and function. Thus, the difficult inverse problem[7](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR7 "Lobo, D., Solano, M., Bubenik, G. A. & Levin, M. A linear-encoding model explains the variability of the target morphology in regeneration. J. R. Soc. Interface 11, 20130918 (2014).") of inferring outcomes and desirable interventions across scales of biology illustrates some of the fundamental questions about the directness or nonlinearity of encodings of information, as well as the importance of this question for practical advances in biomedicine and bioengineering that exploit the plasticity and robustness of cellular collectives. Many deep questions remain about the potential limitations and best strategies to bridge scales for prediction and control in developmental, evolutionary, and cell biology. To that end, we introduce here a formal characterization of the nonlinearity of models of biological regulatory networks, such as those often used to describe relationships between regulatory genes. Specifically, we consider a class of discrete models of biological regulatory systems called “Boolean models” that are known for their relative simplicity and tractability compared to continuous ordinary differential equation-based (ODE) models[8](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR8 "Saadatpour, A. & Albert, R. A comparative study of qualitative and quantitative dynamic models of biological regulatory networks. EPJ Nonlinear Biomed. Phys. 4, 1–13 (2016).").

A Boolean network is a discrete network model characterized by the following features. Each node in a Boolean network can only be in one of two states, ON or OFF, which represents the expression or activity of that node. The state of a node depends on the states of other input nodes which are represented as a Boolean rule of these input nodes. Many of the available Boolean network models were created via literature search of the regulatory mechanisms and subsequently validated via experiments[9](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR9 "Helikar, T., Kowal, B. & Rogers, J. A cell simulator platform: the cell collective. Clin. Pharmacol. Ther. 93, 393–395 (2013)."). Some of the publicly available models were generated via network inference methods from time course data[3](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR3 "Kadelka, C., Butrie, T.-M., Hilton, E., Kinseth, J. & Serdarevic, H. A meta-analysis of Boolean network models reveals design principles of gene regulatory networks. arXiv 
                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2009.01216
                  
                 (2020).").

Previous studies have found that certain characteristic features of the biological Boolean models, such as the mean in-degree, output bias, sensitivity and canalization, tend to assume an optimal range of values that support optimal function[10](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR10 "Daniels, B. C. et al. Criticality distinguishes the ensemble of biological regulatory networks. Phys. Rev. Lett. 121, 138102 (2018)."),[11](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR11 "Manicka, S., Marques-Pita, M. & Rocha, L. M. Effective connectivity determines the critical dynamics of biochemical networks. J. R. Soc. Interface 19, 20210659 (2022)."). Here we study a new but generic feature of complex systems termed “regulatory nonlinearity” that we broadly define as the degree to which the inputs to the components of a complex interact. To characterize the regulatory nonlinearity of Boolean networks we formalize an approach to generalizing Boolean logic by casting it as a form of probability, which was originally proposed by George Boole himself[12](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR12 "Boole, G. Collected Logical Works, Vol. I. Studies in Logic and Probability (ed. Rhees, R.) (Open Court Publishing Company, 1952)."). We leverage the continuous nature of the resulting polynomials to decompose a Boolean function using Taylor-series and reveal the distinct layers of its regulatory nonlinearity (Fig. [5](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig5)). Various other methods, both discrete and continuous, of decomposing Boolean functions exist, such as Reed-Muller, Walsh spectrum, Fourier, discrete Taylor and fuzzy logic[13](#ref-CR13 "O’Donnell, R. Analysis of Boolean Functions (Cambridge University Press, 2014)."),[14](#ref-CR14 "Yanushkevich, S. & Shmerko, V. Taylor expansion of logic functions: from conventional to nanoscale design. In Int. TICSP Workshop on Spectral Methods and Multirate Signal Processing (Citeseer, 2004)."),[15](#ref-CR15 "Grieb, M. et al. Predicting variabilities in cardiac gene expression with a Boolean network incorporating uncertainty. PLoS ONE 10, e0131832 (2015)."),[16](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR16 "Betel, H. & Flocchini, P. On the relationship between fuzzy and Boolean cellular automata. The. Comput. Sci. 412, 703–713 (2011)."). Our continuous Taylor decomposition method is distinct in that it offers a clear and systematic way to characterize nonlinearity.

Is the regulatory nonlinearity of biological systems special? To that end we specifically ask: (1) how well could biological Boolean models be approximated, that is, faithfully represented with only partial information containing lower levels of nonlinearity relative to that of the original?; (2) is there an optimal level of regulatory nonlinearity, characterized by maximum approximability, that these models may have been selected for by evolution?; and (3) do different classes of biological networks show characteristically different optimal levels of regulatory nonlinearity? To answer these questions, we first approximate the biological models by systematically composing the various nonlinear layers resulting in a sequence of model-approximations with increasing levels of nonlinearity. We then estimate the accuracy of these approximations by comparing the outputs of their simulations with that of the original unapproximated model. We then construct an appropriate random ensemble for each biological model and compare their mean accuracies for fixed levels of approximation. The main idea is that a biological model that is more approximable than expected for a particular level of nonlinearity would mean that the network may have been optimized for that level nonlinearity. Finally, we classify the biological networks into various categories and compare their approximabilities to identify any category-dependent effects.

## Results

### The regulation of biological models tends to be less nonlinear than expected by chance

The mean approximation error (MAE; see “Methods”) of biological models tends to be less than both the constrained and the unconstrained random ensembles across all approximation orders (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)a). At the linear order, for example, the mean MAE of biological models is about 0.025, whereas the mean MAE of the constrained ensembles is twice as large at about 0.05 (*p* < 10−6) and even larger for the unconstrained ensembles at about 0.07 (*p* < 10−23). This ordering of MAE extends to all approximation orders (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)a). Even though the mean MAE of the unconstrained ensembles tends to be remarkably low (0.07 at worst), the percentage change in MAE (PMAE; see “Methods”) convey a more accurate sense of approximability. For example, the MAE of the biological models and the constrained ensembles are respectively about 50 and 25% lower than the baseline expectation for the linear order (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)b). This means that the biological models are about 25% more linearly approximable than expected by chance, an effect that amplifies with higher approximation orders going up to 50% for order 4, since the PMAE of biological models shrinks faster than the constrained ensembles (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)b).

A possible explanation for the observed trends is that the Taylor spectrums of the biological models tend to be lopsided and more clustered at the linear ends (see Fig. [4](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig4) for an example), dramatically reducing the load on the higher orders and thus resulting in a faster growth of approximability with higher orders. This effect may be less pronounced in constrained ensembles, and even less in unconstrained ensembles, as the apportioning in the corresponding Taylor spectrums may be less lopsided and more uniform in comparison. Based on these observations we hypothesize, but do not conclude, that biological systems may have been subjected to a slight but discernible selection pressure for developing less nonlinear regulatory rules. This hypothesis is further supported by the fact that the approximability systematically increases as more biological constraints are imposed, as one goes from unconstrained to constrained to the biological models (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)). This has implications not only for the feasibility of biomedical approaches to control emergent somatic complexity or guided self-assembly of novel forms[17](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR17 "Ebrahimkhani, M. R. & Levin, M. Synthetic living machines: a new window on life. Iscience 24, 102505 (2021)."), but also for models of anatomical homeostasis and evolvability: linearity implies easier control of its own complex processes by any biological system, and more efficient credit assignment during evolution.

### Random models with minimal biological constraints tend to be naturally approximable

The MAE of the unconstrained random ensembles are remarkably low with values averaging around 0.07 and not exceeding 0.17 at the linear order; these values only decrease further with higher approximation orders (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)a). These observations suggest that even random Boolean models with minimal biological constraints tend to be considerably approximable. In other words, approximability may be a natural property of random Boolean ensembles to some extent and not necessarily a special property of biological models. Furthermore, the MAE has its inflection point at order 2 (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)a), meaning that the approximability starts saturating at that order. We hypothesize based on these observations that regulatory information beyond the second order may be inconsequential with regards to the statistics of network dynamics in general.

The above hypothesis has an analog in the realm of Boolean Ising models, where it was found that maximum entropy (MaxEnt) models with only pairwise interactions were sufficient to fit random multivariate Ising networks that are densely connected and whose state spaces satisfy certain entropy constraints, features that many biological systems share[18](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR18 "Merchan, L. & Nemenman, I. On the sufficiency of pairwise interactions in maximum entropy models of networks. J. Stat. Phys. 162, 1294–1308 (2016)."). There are however important differences between our results and theirs: (1) even though the Taylor expansions resemble MaxEnt expressions, the latter fit global state-space distributions, whereas our Taylor polynomials are local formulations; and (2) whereas our Taylor polynomials are built on derivatives of the states, MaxEnt models are based on the raw values. Notwithstanding these potentially superficial differences, the analogous results are striking and calls for further research on whether the respective explanations are similar if not fundamentally the same. Besides, the above observations are reminiscent of the concept of model degeneracy or “sloppiness”, where several models (defined by unique sets of parameters) explain the same biological phenomenon due to redundant parameters[19](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR19 "Gutenkunst, R. N. et al. Universally sloppy parameter sensitivities in systems biology models. PLoS Comput. Biol. 3, e189 (2007)."). In our case, it’s not the parameters but the higher-order relationships among them that are redundant, as they contribute minimally to the MAE beyond a certain order of approximation (Fig. [2](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig2)a). Future research will determine whether there’s a deep connection between the sloppiness of the parameters and the orders of relationships among them.

### The approximability of a biological model depends on its class, with the cancer family displaying the most variability

Even though the nonlinearity of biological networks is less than expected on average, individual and category-dependent variations were observed. In the following, we focus on the “linear approximability” (PMAE corresponding to the linear order) of the biological models since it’s hypothesized to be the cause of the observed approximabilities in the higher order; values are listed in Supplementary Table [1](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#MOESM1). First, there are a few networks in almost every category that are more nonlinear than expected, as evidenced by the negative linear approximability values (Fig. [3](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig3)). Second, the disease networks in C1 and the cancer networks in C2 are the ones with the most linear approximability, with values ≥75% (Fig. [3](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig3)). In other words, the cancer or disease pathways tend to be more optimized for linearity compared to the other categories. This makes sense since a more linear pathway is more amenable to control, which presumably works in favor of the agents of disease. On the other hand, the disease and cancer networks also display the highest variability in their linear approximability compared to other categories (*p* < 0.05 in all comparisons for the cancer models), with the PMAE values ranging between −92 and 80% (Fig. [3](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig3)). In other words, disease and cancer models could be either relatively extremely more linear or nonlinear than expected, depending on the specific cases. Note that about 56% of the disease category comprises of non-cancer networks (31/55), which suggests that the effect is not significantly biased by cancer networks. These observations suggest that regulatory nonlinearity may offer an effective “entry point” to the agents of disease by virtue of its natural heterogeneity that they could leverage to their advantage perhaps as a means to evade treatment since there’s no single level of nonlinearity to target. This heterogeneity may also have a connection to one of the hallmarks of cancer, namely genetic heterogeneity[20](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR20 "Hanahan, D. & Weinberg, R. A. Hallmarks of cancer: the next generation. Cell 144, 646–674 (2011).") where the cancerous cells within an individual display heterogeneous gene expression compared to the homogeneous expression in the healthy cells. In the case of nonlinearity, the heterogeneity manifests at the population level, raising the question of whether it may also be observed at the level of single cells within an individual. In other words, could the heterogeneity of nonlinearity be yet another hallmark of cancer?

### The shape of the Taylor spectrum explains the extreme opposite characters of linear approximability of a pair of cancer models

Why are some models more linearly approximable and others less? The answer lies in the organization of the corresponding Taylor decompositions, as described above. To illustrate this in detail, we compared the Taylor decompositions of a pair of models chosen from among the most extreme outliers of linear approximability in either direction (Fig. [3](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig3)). Those models are respectively the following: a linear model describing the role of the protein p53 in the regulation of cell-cycle arrest in breast cancer[21](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR21 "Choi, M., Shi, J., Jung, S. H., Chen, X. & Cho, K.-H. Attractor landscape analysis reveals feedback loops in the p53 network that control the cellular response to dna damage. Sci. Signal. 5, ra83–ra83 (2012)."), henceforth referred to as the “P53” model; and a nonlinear model describing the role of mutations in the regulation of metastasis in lung cancer[22](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR22 "Cohen, D. P. et al. Mathematical modelling of molecular pathways enabling tumour cell invasion and migration. PLoS Comput. Biol. 11, e1004571 (2015)."), henceforth referred to as the “Metastasis” model. P53 has a linear approximability of about 72%, and it consists of 16 nodes with a mean in-degree of 3.8 ± 2.4 and a mean output bias of 0.38 ± 0.14; and, Metastasis has a linear approximability of about −79%, and it consists of 32 nodes with a mean in-degree of 4.9 ± 2.5 and a mean output bias of 0.27 ± 0.26. Thus, while P53 is smaller and sparser than Metastasis, its nodes exhibit more output-uncertainty compared to Metastasis. According to the mean field theory of random Boolean networks[23](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR23 "Derrida, B. & Pomeau, Y. Random networks of automata: a simple annealed approximation. Europhys. Lett. 1, 45 (1986)."), the opposite characters of the mean in-degree and the output bias of these models means that their dynamical behaviors could be expected to be similar (although with the caution that the theory was originally developed for infinite-sized and homogeneously connected networks, which is not the case here). However, we know that their linear approximabilities, which is another expression of dynamical behavior, are opposites. One explanation for this discrepancy lies in the distinct apportioning of nonlinearity in their respective Taylor decompositions (Fig. [4](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig4)). Specifically, while the magnitude of nonlinearity, defined as the mean absolute value of the Taylor derivatives for a given order and normalized appropriately (see text of Fig. [4](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig4)), tends to be clustered around the linear order for P53, they are relatively more spread out for Metastasis. Moreover, while the magnitude of the linear order for P53 is more than twice as large the next largest magnitude at lower orders the corresponding ratios for Metastasis are relatively smaller, thus explaining why P53 is more linearly approximable than Metastasis. This result is consistent with predictions based on a model of scaling of cellular control policies[24](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR24 "Levin, M. Bioelectrical approaches to cancer as a problem of the scaling of the cellular self. Prog. Biophys. Mol. Biol. 165, 102–113 (2021)."). A more controllable (linear) network (P53) is optimal for cooperation with other cells toward collective (normal morphogenetic) goals. In contrast, a cell defecting from the collective and reverting to a more unicellular lifestyle (Metastasis) should exhibit a less predictable, controllable network due to pressures from parasites and competitors that independent unicellular organisms face. Methods for calculating controllability (e.g., linearity) are an important addition to recent efforts to solve the conundrum of interpretability of information structures in contexts ranging from machine learning to evolutionary developmental biology[25](#ref-CR25 "Linardatos, P., Papastefanopoulos, V. & Kotsiantis, S. Explainable AI: a review of machine learning interpretability methods. Entropy 23, 18 (2020)."),[26](#ref-CR26 "Manicka, S. & Levin, M. Minimal developmental computation: a causal network approach to understand morphogenetic pattern formation. Entropy 24, 107 (2022)."),[27](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR27 "Watson, R. A., Levin, M. & Buckley, C. L. Design for an individual: connectionist approaches to the evolutionary transitions in individuality. Front. Ecol. Evol. 64, 64–87 (2022).").

## Discussion

This paper introduces the concept of regulatory nonlinearity as a characteristic of Boolean networks. There are several other related characterizations of Boolean networks such as canalization[28](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR28 "Kauffman, S. The large scale structure and dynamics of gene control circuits: an ensemble approach. J. Theor. Biol. 44, 167–190 (1974)."), effective connectivity[11](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR11 "Manicka, S., Marques-Pita, M. & Rocha, L. M. Effective connectivity determines the critical dynamics of biochemical networks. J. R. Soc. Interface 19, 20210659 (2022)."), symmetry[29](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR29 "Reichhardt, C. O. & Bassler, K. E. Canalization and symmetry in Boolean models for genetic regulatory networks. J. Phys. A 40, 4339 (2007).") and controllability[30](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR30 "Gates, A. J. & Rocha, L. M. Control of complex networks requires both structure and dynamics. Sci. Rep. 6, 1–11 (2016)."). It has been previously reported that the levels of canalization (a measure of the extent to which a subset of inputs actually influence the outputs of a Boolean function) and the mean effective connectivity (a measure of collective canalization) are high in biological networks[3](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR3 "Kadelka, C., Butrie, T.-M., Hilton, E., Kinseth, J. & Serdarevic, H. A meta-analysis of Boolean network models reveals design principles of gene regulatory networks. arXiv 
                  https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2009.01216
                  
                 (2020)."),[11](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR11 "Manicka, S., Marques-Pita, M. & Rocha, L. M. Effective connectivity determines the critical dynamics of biochemical networks. J. R. Soc. Interface 19, 20210659 (2022)."). It has also been found that biological networks need few inputs to reprogram[31](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR31 "Müller, F.-J. & Schuppert, A. Few inputs can reprogram biological networks. Nature 478, E4–E4 (2011).") and are relatively easier to control[4](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR4 "Borriello, E. & Daniels, B. C. The basis of easy controllability in Boolean networks. Nat. Commun. 12, 5227 (2021)."). Our formulation of regulatory nonlinearity is related to these other measures in that more linearity implies more apportioning of influence to individual inputs rather than collective sets of inputs (Fig. [4](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#Fig4)). Hence, we hypothesize that regulatory nonlinearity may serve the purpose of controllability and epigenetic stability[32](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR32 "Wagner, A. Does evolutionary plasticity evolve? Evolution 50, 1008–1023 (1996).").

Our results further moot the possibility that regulatory nonlinearity may be a factor underlying more powerful dynamical phenomena such as memory[33](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR33 "Biswas, S., Manicka, S., Hoel, E. & Levin, M. Gene regulatory networks exhibit several kinds of memory: quantification of memory in biological and random transcriptional networks. Iscience 24, 102131 (2021).") and computation, defined as the capacity for adaptive information-processing[34](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR34 "Mitchell, M. Ubiquity 2011, 1-7. Ubiquity 2011, 1–7 (2011)."). Even though there’s increasing consensus that biological systems contain memory and perform computation, clarity is lacking as to what features of those systems enable it and what general principles underlie it[34](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR34 "Mitchell, M. Ubiquity 2011, 1-7. Ubiquity 2011, 1–7 (2011)."),[35](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR35 "Chu, D., Prokopenko, M. & Ray, J. C. J. Computation by natural systems (2018)."). Our framework of regulatory nonlinearity offers an approach to answering these questions. For example, one could consider a known dynamical model with a capacity for memory[33](/articles/s41540-023-00273-w#ref-CR33 "Biswas, S., Manicka, S., Hoel, E. & Levin, M. Gene regulatory networks exhibit several kinds of memory: quantification of me

--- SOURCE 2: Mastering Non-Linear Dynamics for Biological Insights ---

SUMMARY:
In this article, we will explore the application of non-linear dynamics and linear algebra to analyze complex biological systems.

by SW Jarantow · 2023 · Cited by 61 — The inherent complexity involved in evaluating a biological system requires the use of rigorous and appropriate tools for data analysis. Linear and nonlinear

# Introduction to the Use of Linear and Nonlinear Regression Analysis in Quantitative Biological Assays

# Introduction to the Use of Linear and Nonlinear Regression Analysis in Quantitative Biological Assays

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Biological assays are essential tools in biomedical and pharmaceutical research. In simplest terms, such an assay is an analytical method used to measure or predict a response in a biological system in the presence of a given stimulus (e.g., drug). The inherent complexity involved in evaluating a biological system requires the use of rigorous and appropriate tools for data analysis. Linear and nonlinear regression models represent critically important statistical analyses used to define the relationships between variables of interest in biological systems. Recent challenges relating to the reproducibility of published data suggest the absence of standardized and routine use of statistics to support experimental results across a wide range of scientific disciplines. The current situation warrants an introductory review of basic regression concepts using current, practical examples, along with references to in-depth resources. The goal is to provide the necessary information to help standardize the analysis of biological assays in academic research and drug discovery and development, elevating their utility and increasing data transparency and reproducibility. © 2023 The Authors. Current Protocols published by Wiley Periodicals LLC.

## Abstract

Biological assays are essential tools in biomedical and pharmaceutical research. In simplest terms, such an assay is an analytical method used to measure or predict a response in a biological system in the presence of a given stimulus (e.g., drug). The inherent complexity involved in evaluating a biological system requires the use of rigorous and appropriate tools for data analysis. Linear and nonlinear regression models represent critically important statistical analyses used to define the relationships between variables of interest in biological systems. Recent challenges relating to the reproducibility of published data suggest the absence of standardized and routine use of statistics to support experimental results across a wide range of scientific disciplines. The current situation warrants an introductory review of basic regression concepts using current, practical examples, along with references to in-depth resources. The goal is to provide the necessary information to help standardize the analysis of biological assays in academic research and drug discovery and development, elevating their utility and increasing data transparency and reproducibility. © 2023 The Authors. Current Protocols published by Wiley Periodicals LLC.

## INTRODUCTION

### Biological Assays

Biological assays, or bioassays, constitute a broad array of experiments designed to measure the effects of a substance when it is applied to living matter, such as cells, tissues, or organisms (Grimm, [1979](#cpz1801-bib-0074); Panuganti, [2015b](#cpz1801-bib-0145)). In drug discovery, bioassays are used to evaluate the pharmacological activity of substances in all stages of development, from newly discovered and uncharacterized entities to medicines approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and other government bodies. Most bioassays estimate the potency, quality, stability, or concentration of a test substance by examining one or more predefined responses that they elicit. Bioassays can be used to assess a substance's therapeutic potential, elucidate or reflect its mechanism of action (MOA), diagnose disease, or monitor product consistency. Additionally, environmental and agricultural scientists use bioassays to gauge the potential toxicity of substances to human health, as well as to monitor their effects on terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems (Lyne et al., [1992](#cpz1801-bib-0109); Markwiese et al., [2001](#cpz1801-bib-0113)). Examples of test substances include, but are not limited to: drugs, natural products, chemicals, enzymes, hormones, antibodies, peptides, small molecules (e.g., antibiotics), cells (e.g., CAR-T), and energy (e.g., radiation). Typically, test substances are applied to living matter under a standardized set of conditions and delivered in one or more well-defined doses. The main goal of a bioassay is therefore to elucidate the relationship between the applied dose and the measured response.

The potential number of measurable responses used in bioassays is limited only by their utility and capacity for validation. The measured response is a function of, and invariably incorporates, what is known or feasible regarding that particular drug candidate's MOA. Generalized examples of common MOAs incorporated into bioassays include, but are not limited to, agonism/antagonism of soluble targets (i.e., ligand, cytokine) or cell surface receptors, effects on cell-cell or protein-protein interactions, signal transduction events (i.e., kinase activity, protein phosphorylation, protein translocation, DNA methylation, transcription/translation), antigenicity, neutralization, proliferation, cellular viability/toxicity, cellular migration, morphological changes in cells or tissue (i.e., muscle contraction), and mortality. Bioassays are also increasingly being utilized in the field of pharmacogenomics, the study of how individual genetics contribute to a drug response ([Welsh et al., 2009)](#cpz1801-bib-0208).

Ideally, the measured response is evaluated against a standard of known potency, quality, or concentration, and whose MOA has been well defined and evaluated under identical experimental conditions. Due to the difficulty of measuring the true potency or activity of any given substance in isolation, bioassays are generally both designed and valued for their capacity for comparison. For example, diabetes researchers seeking to identify insulin mimetics from a panel of candidate compounds may use a *glucose uptake assay* to examine the ability of a drug candidate to trigger the absorption of radiolabeled glucose by differentiated adipocytes. They may compare the potency of their candidate(s) relative to a standard of care (e.g., insulin) or to a commercial competitor. Comparisons of this kind are frequently referred to as measures of relative potency. Relative potency is an important component in the analysis of bioassays and will be described in greater detail in the section on linear regression.

Bioassays are complex and challenging to design, validate, execute, and analyze. Due to their use of living matter, complex media and reagents, and often intricate and lengthy protocols, bioassays are by their nature subject to great variability. Yet they represent a fundamental and ubiquitous tool in academic research, drug discovery, and clinical development, as well as quality control environments involving product and process development and manufacturing (Little, [2016](#cpz1801-bib-0104)). Bioassays are also dynamic: They must continually coevolve in their scope alongside advancements in platform technology (e.g., high content imaging, NGS), molecular design (e.g., fragment crystallizable (Fc) engineering, fit-for-purpose biologics), and drug targeting strategies (e.g., multispecific agents with more than one MOA, CART-T, gene therapy) (Quintarelli et al., [2016](#cpz1801-bib-0156); Ramsay et al., [2018](#cpz1801-bib-0157); Strohl, [2018](#cpz1801-bib-0186)).

Well-designed bioassays thus serve many purposes: (1) facilitating the identification of novel drugs and characterizing their therapeutic potential; (2) defining the mechanisms by which those drugs exert their therapeutic potential; (3) evaluating potential drug toxicity and helping to define pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling strategies (Danhof et al., [2008](#cpz1801-bib-0040)); (4) comparing the activity of new drugs to those of existing drugs (i.e., generics, biosimilars); (5) helping to diagnose disease and monitor disease progression (i.e., clinical biomarkers); and (6) standardizing of drug manufacturing by demonstrating product stability, purity, equivalency, or consistency of the material being produced (Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research, [2008](#cpz1801-bib-0030); Shao, [2009](#cpz1801-bib-0174)).

To avoid confusion, it is worth noting that within the pharmaceutical industry the term bioassay characteristically implies *in vitro* cell-based assays, although in its broadest definition the term “bioassay” does embrace *in vitro*, *ex vivo*, and *in vivo* experiments, including animal toxicity, translational data, and human clinical trials (van Mil, [2008](#cpz1801-bib-0200)). This review will likewise focus primarily on *in vitro* bioassays.

Bioassays are intended to be specific, predictive, fast, reproducible, and cost-effective. *In vitro* bioassays with these qualities are especially valued and well-positioned in industry because they do not pose the same ethical challenges, time constraints, and costs as *in vivo* models. R&D discovery programs often need to identify one or two leads for development from candidate panels of tens to perhaps thousands of candidates. *In vitro* bioassays, though not a replacement or substitute for *in vivo* testing, can therefore serve as a critical decision point in helping to filter out candidates with less desirable functional attributes. In this manner bioassays embody the fast-paced nature of drug discovery, which rewards R&D competitors able to minimize development time to achieve first-in-class and breakthrough status for new therapeutics. The availability of well-characterized cell lines, such as the NCI-60 and tissues of relevant disease origin, provide a huge, modern resource for bioassay development, and can help elucidate and potentially inform *in vivo* behavior at a greatly reduced time and cost expenditure relative to clinical studies (Abaan et al., [2013](#cpz1801-bib-0001)).

For an introduction to the concept of bioassays and their general design, see the review by Panuganti ([2015b](#cpz1801-bib-0145)). For in-depth references on bioassay examples and methodologies, see the texts by Choudhary and Thomsen ([2001](#cpz1801-bib-0034)) and Peng and Zhao ([2009](#cpz1801-bib-0147)), or guidance documents published by Markossian ([2004](#cpz1801-bib-0112)), Blume et al. ([2011](#cpz1801-bib-0021)), the Food and Drug Administration ([2001](#cpz1801-bib-0077)), ICH ([2016](#cpz1801-bib-0087)), and The United States Pharmacopeia ([2023a](#cpz1801-bib-0195),b,c). For an overview of considerations for bioassays that directly support the development and commercialization of a drug, see the white paper by White et al. ([2019](#cpz1801-bib-0210)). For an overview specifically detailing environmental bioassays, refer to the text by Erzinger and Häder ([2017](#cpz1801-bib-0051)), while more theory-based texts on environmental biotechnology and toxicology that include relevant bioassay considerations include those by Mohapatra ([2008](#cpz1801-bib-0124)), Van Gestel et al. ([2019](#cpz1801-bib-0199)).

### Types of Bioassays

Biological assays are frequently divided into two main categories, quantitative and qualitative. Quantitative bioassays, the emphasis of this review, allow scientists to draw statistically measurable conclusions about activity, physical properties or concentration. Quantitative bioassays are generally subdivided into three types. (1) Direct assays involve the administration of a dose until a predefined response is reached (e.g., intravenous delivery until mortality is reached). The nature of their format restricts the use of regression for analysis. (2) Indirect quantitative assays measure continuous and random responses across a predetermined, continuous dosage, and are the format most amenable to linear and nonlinear regression (Le & Eberly, [2016](#cpz1801-bib-0098); Panuganti, [2015a](#cpz1801-bib-0144)). Indirect quantitative assays will provide examples for this review. (3) Indirect quantal (all-or-nothing) assays involve a predefined dose range, but a binary response (e.g., LD50, the dose where 50% mortality is reached). Quantal assays will not be discussed in detail in this review, but typically involve a generalized linear model of regression called logistic regression, discussed in the texts by Cook et al. ([2001](#cpz1801-bib-0037)), Kleinbaum and Klein ([2010](#cpz1801-bib-0095)), and Morgan ([2005](#cpz1801-bib-0127)). Strictly qualitative assays, which also display binary readouts, for example confirming the presence or absence of a substance (e.g., detection of a DNA polymorphism), are often used both in early-stage discovery and process development, but they do not raise statistical endpoint challenges, and therefore will not be discussed here (Grimm, [1979](#cpz1801-bib-0074); Wild, [2016](#cpz1801-bib-0212)).

Because bioassays are conducted on living cells or tissues, they are inherently complex and prone to variability; therefore, good experimental design and statistical analysis are essential to achieving useful results (Roberts & Boyce, [1972](#cpz1801-bib-0164)). Interpretation of the dose-response relationship can help explain and predict toxicity, rank order drug candidates, define drug behavior, and assist modeling strategies for dosing studies. Although methodological differences arise depending on the scientific discipline involved and the type of bioassay performed, the principles required to achieve significant and reproducible experimental results should ideally be universal.

### Reproducibility Crisis

For some time, there has been an apparent crisis in the scientific community regarding the low reproducibility of experimental data (Begley & Ellis, [2012](#cpz1801-bib-0012); Flier, [2017](#cpz1801-bib-0057); Ioannidis, [2005](#cpz1801-bib-0088); MacCoun & Perlmutter, [2015](#cpz1801-bib-0110); Nature, [2016](#cpz1801-bib-0136); Nuzzo, [2015](#cpz1801-bib-0139)). According to a 2016 *Nature* survey, over 70% of researchers have been unable to reproduce another scientist's experiments, and more than 50% have been unable to reproduce their own experiments (Baker, [2016](#cpz1801-bib-0009)). This crisis is frequently described within the context of data integrity, a term that encompasses all the requirements for completeness, traceability, and accuracy of recorded data throughout the life cycle of a drug product, including considerations for the sophistications of the modern digital age (Rattan, [2018](#cpz1801-bib-0159); Ruth, [2017](#cpz1801-bib-0167)). Although the reasons for the reproducibility crisis are manifold, both poor experimental design and low statistical strength of the data are often cited as important contributing factors. The pharmaceutical industry, public and private research institutions, peer-reviewed journals, and regulatory agencies have all recognized and responded to this crisis through multiple measures. Recent initiatives include incentivizing transparency, enhancing oversight, and standardizing protocols and reporting measures through open-access databases and specific guidelines issued by scientific journals (Bolton, [2015](#cpz1801-bib-0022); Olsen, [2014](#cpz1801-bib-0140); Prescott & Civil, [2013](#cpz1801-bib-0152); Reiss, [2017](#cpz1801-bib-0160); Worthy, [2015](#cpz1801-bib-0214)). Table [1](#cpz1801-tbl-0001 "Link to table") describes several key projects begun toward addressing the reproducibility crisis. A review of the current best practices for statistical analysis in quantitative bioassays, as described here, reinforces the groundwork for change as the scientific community embraces these new initiatives, whose importance cannot be overstated.

Table 1.
Initiatives to Address the Reproducibility Challenges in Scientific Research

| Name | Founded | Founder(s) | Goal |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| PubChem | 2004 | NIH Molecular Libraries Program (MLP)/Consortium | Open database of chemical molecules and biological activities (<https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/>) |
| Protocol Exchange | 2006 | Nature | Open source repository for protocols for scientific research (<https://www.nature.com/protocolexchange/>) |

| Ontology for Biomedical Investigations (OBI) | 2008 | Consortium | Open source ontology for defining all aspects of biological and biomedical experiments (<http://obi-ontology.org/>) |
| ChEMBL | 2009 | EMBL-EBI | Open source database of binding, functional and ADMET bioactivity data (<https://www.ebi.ac.uk/chembl/>) |

## INTRODUCTION TO REGRESSION

Not surprisingly, efforts to interpret, standardize, or improve the quality of bioassay data demand a thorough statistical assessment. Solutions to such challenges are satisfied by the field of biostatistics, an enormous discipline describing the application of statistical theories and mathematics to the interpretation of biological data. Biostatistics encompasses experimental design, data analysis, and data interpretation, with applications in diverse fields such as medicine, epidemiology, genetics, ecology, and agriculture. Relevant, comprehensive publications in biostatistics include an introductory handbook by McDonald ([2014](#cpz1801-bib-0118)) designed for the biologist; canonical texts by Grimm ([1979](#cpz1801-bib-0074)), Sokal and Rohlf ([1995](#cpz1801-bib-0180)), and Zar ([1999](#cpz1801-bib-0217)); as well as work emphasizing complex biological models by Quinn and Keough ([2002](#cpz1801-bib-0155)). This review will introduce a single, but important, biostatistical technique, that of regression analysis. To emphasize the difference in scale, as biostatisticians use the power of statistics to try and interpret any biological question, here we introduce the appropriate use of a single statistical approach to one critical type of experimental platform.

Although each experiment involves unique considerations, the use of regression analysis is an essential component of the meaningful interpretation of biological data (Motulsky & Christopoulos, [2004](#cpz1801-bib-0130)). In simplest terms, regression analysis is a statistical method used to evaluate the relationship between at least two continuous variables. Typically, bioassays define a dependent, or response, variable (*Y*) and one or more independent (predictor or explanatory) variables (*X*) to which the dependent variable responds. Such experiments seek to measure the response in *Y* when *X* is defined and systematically altered. In defining the relationship between or among variables, regression provides a computational model against which the data generated are compared. The scientist then uses a variety of statistical parameters to assess how well that model fits the data. Regression can provide the ability to predict values for one variable based on the values of the other. And, as cited in the introduction, a frequent goal of biological assays is to assess the potency of candidate molecules in exerting a desired effect. Regression quantifies the observed behavior of these molecules, allowing for statistically validated comparisons of the desired biological activity.

The most frequent types of regression analyses applied to biological assays are termed linear and nonlinear regression. As we shall see, the comparative analysis of multiple molecules through binding or activity profiles in a biological system most frequently involves nonlinear analysis, whereas linear methods are typically applied for the determination of unknowns (The United States Pharmacopeia, [2023b](#cpz1801-bib-0196); Villeneuve et al., [2000](#cpz1801-bib-0201)). In this review, we focus on the principles of curve-fitting from the perspective of a research scientist designing and conducting the experiments, without an emphasis on the underlying mathematical formulae. Biological researchers today have access to a wide range of general and specialized statistical software platforms to facilitate bioassay analysis. Table [2](#cpz1801-tbl-0002 "Link to table") provides a list of some programs commonly used in research, chosen for their suitability in the analysis of bioassays. They differ in functionality, ease of use, cost, and intended audience. For example, there is a distinction between statistical software written for statisticians (i.e., R), and those written for scientists with only fundamental statistical training (i.e., GraphPad Prism). Every discipline has its own constraints and skillsets. If one surveyed various scientists—a T cell biologist in discovery research, a non-clinical biostatistician, a bioanalytical scientist in a cGMP facility, and a graduate student in epidemiology—the preferred software choice of each is unlikely to be the same. Although instructional resources for individual statistical software packages abound online, recent, comparative reviews of statistical software do not. Some insight can be obtained from articles by De Leeuw ([2011](#cpz1801-bib-0045)) and Johnson ([2014](#cpz1801-bib-0091)) or in the open-access *Journal of Statistical Software*.

Here, we present an outline for a simple, strong statistical introduction to the proper use of simple regression models, providing multiple examples of bioassays for each application, recommendations for goodness-of-fit and acceptance criteria, and illustrations of how scientists can extract meaningful interpretations of the results. For an in-depth statistical approach to regression analysis with examples, see Chatterjee and Hadi ([2012](#cpz1801-bib-0033)). Draper ([1998](#cpz1801-bib-0046)), Seber ([1977](#cpz1801-bib-0172)), and Montgomery et al. ([2012](#cpz1801-bib-0126)) focus on solutions in linear regression, while essential texts on nonlinear regression include Bates and Watts ([1988](#cpz1801-bib-0011)), Gallant ([1987](#cpz1801-bib-0062)), and Seber and Wild ([2005](#cpz1801-bib-0173)).

### Linear Regression

If regression analysis generally describes a statistical examination of the relationship between two variables, linear regression entails finding the best-fit straight line through a series of data points when that relationship is assumed to be linear. A linear relationship is the simplest type possible between two variables, and states that for every change in one variable there occurs a consistent and corresponding change in another variable. The fact that linear regression assumes a linear relationship exists is a simple, yet important, point to reiterate when considering the best model for a prospective assay.

Linear regression is the most widely used modeling method, and for research scientists, its utility rests in its predictive capabilities (Croarkin & Tobias, [2004](#cpz1801-bib-0039); National Institute of Standards and Technology, [2000](#cpz1801-bib-0135)). Colorimetric protein assays (i.e., Bradford, Lowry) are well-known examples of linear regression used for the quantitation of protein in unknown samples (Sapan et al., [2010](#cpz1801-bib-0169); Simonian & Smith, [2006](#cpz1801-bib-0176); Stoscheck, [1990](#cpz1801-bib-0185)). These assays use chemicals that undergo spectroscopic shifts proportional to the amount of protein present, enabling linear comparisons between absorbance and concentration. However, biological data, especially dose-response data, rarely adhere to a strictly linear relationship and instead more often demonstrate curvilinear or nonlinear behavior (Brace, [1977](#cpz1801-bib-0023); Villeneuve et al., [2000](#cpz1801-bib-0201)). Often, linear relationships are valid and qualified within a limited response range in a bioassay. The Bradford assay, in fact, has only a narrow linear range for accurately measuring protein concentration (Zor & Selinger, [1996](#cpz1801-bib-0219)). Bioassays frequently involve data transformations that induce linearity. Such data transformations (e.g., nonlinear transformations) can potentially alter the data in a manner that violates the assumptions of linear regression (discussed later) and should therefore be evaluated cautiously (Davidian & Haaland, [2008](#cpz1801-bib-0043)). Although nonlinear regression models offer more versatility than linear regression for most biological assays, and can even be used to analyze linear data, linear regression is the logical starting point for this review because it is conceptually simpler, remains in common use, and represents a foundational method for extensions into more complex analyses (James et al., [2017](#cpz1801-bib-0089); Rencher & Schaalje, [2008](#cpz1801-bib-0161); [Twomey & Kroll, 2008)](#cpz1801-bib-0194).

### Ordinary Least-Squares (Olsen)

The goal of simple linear regression is to identify a straight line through a series of points (*i* = 1, *i* + 1, …, *n*) that best approximates the relationship between the dependent (*Y*) and independent (*X*) variables. As a starting point, assume that there exists no relationship between *X* and *Y*. A scatterplot (Fig. [1A](#cpz1801-fig-0001)) of the observed data would then be completely random, and the best value for *Y* that could be inferred from every value of *X* would be the population mean of *Y*
. Now, consider a straight-line function as shown in Figure [1B](#cpz1801-fig-0001) and represented by the familiar equation,

(1)

where is the predicted dependent variables (for the *i*th observation), is the independent variables, β1 is the slope of the line (Δ*Y*/Δ*X*) and β0 is the *y*-intercept parameter. In Figure [1B](#cpz1801-fig-0001), the data points all fall in a straight line, the slope and *y*-intercept are precisely measurable, and the relationship between *X* and *Y* is consistent and perfectly linear, such that knowing *X* allows you to calculate *Y* with 100% certainty. *Y* as represented in Equation 1 indicates the true value of *Y*. enriched_points.expanded_content: Biological systems exhibit complex non-linear interactions, influencing regulatory dynamics and emergent properties, necessitating advanced modeling techniques for accurate representation and prediction.
Recent advancements in computational tools, including probabilistic Boolean models, enhance the analysis of non-linear biological interactions, improving the understanding of regulatory networks.

Machine learning techniques are increasingly applied to biological data, enabling the extraction of patterns and insights from complex datasets, thus revolutionizing trait discovery and biological interpretation.
